segments.inferno-15-g53: “El” is an archaic/poetic form for “he,” referring to the speaker introduced by the surrounding narrative. “Cominciò” is the third-person singular passato remoto of “cominciare,” used as a narrative past: “he began.”
segments.inferno-15-g54: “Qual fortuna o destino” is an interrogative subject phrase, literally “what fortune or destiny,” with “qual” asking what force is responsible. “Anzi l’ultimo dì” means “before the last/final day”; here “dì” is the old noun for “day,” though the supplied lemma lists the verb “dire.” “Qua giù ti mena” has object pronoun “ti” before the verb: “leads you down here.”
segments.inferno-15-g55: This continues the direct question begun in the previous sentence: “e” links a second question to the first. “Questi” is a demonstrative pronoun meaning “this one,” and the relative clause “che mostra ’l cammino” identifies him as the one who shows or points out the path; “’l” is the contracted form of “il/lo,” here “the.”
segments.inferno-15-g56: This segment contains only punctuation, marking the close of the quoted question. There are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-15-g57: “Là sù di sopra” is a piled-up adverbial expression meaning “up there above,” pointing to the world of the living as opposed to Hell below. The phrase is placed first for emphasis, setting the location before the main verb of Dante’s reply.
segments.inferno-15-g58: “In la vita serena” uses the uncontracted “in la,” where modern Italian would normally have “nella.” The phrase means “in the serene life,” a poetic way of referring to earthly life under the clear, calm sky.
segments.inferno-15-g59: “Rispuos’” is an elided form of “rispuosi,” first-person singular passato remoto: “I replied.” The subject “io” follows the verb, a common poetic/narrative inversion, and “lui” functions as the indirect object, “to him.”
segments.inferno-15-g60: “Mi smarri’” is an elided form of “mi smarrii,” with reflexive “mi”: literally “I lost myself,” idiomatically “I went astray” or “lost my way.” “In una valle” gives the place where this happened, echoing Dante’s earlier dark valley/wood imagery.
segments.inferno-15-g61: “Avanti che” means “before” and takes the imperfect subjunctive “fosse,” as is normal after such temporal conjunctions when the event is viewed as not yet realized at the reference point. “L’età mia fosse piena” literally means “my age/lifetime was full,” i.e. before Dante had reached the full measure of his life.
segments.inferno-15-g24: This segment contains only a full stop, marking the close of the preceding direct speech; there are no words to parse here.
segments.inferno-15-g25: “E io” introduces Dante as the subject, but the main verb is delayed until “ficcaï” and then “rispuosi.” This suspended word order is poetic and lets the intervening temporal clause describe the moment of recognition.
segments.inferno-15-g26: “Quando” introduces a temporal subordinate clause, “when he stretched his arm toward me.” “’l” is a contraction of “il,” and “distese” is a past definite form of “distendere,” here with the subject implied as Brunetto.
segments.inferno-15-g27: “Ficcaï” is a poetic spelling of “ficcai,” first-person past definite: “I fixed” or “I drove” my eyes. “Per lo cotto aspetto” means Dante looks through or into the scorched outward appearance, with “lo” as the older masculine article form.
segments.inferno-15-g28: “Sì che” forms a result clause: “so that.” The subject is “’l viso abbrusciato,” and “non difese” means “did not prevent”; the object is “la conoscenza süa,” recognition of him, from reaching or occurring in Dante’s “’ntelletto,” an apocopated form of “intelletto.”
segments.inferno-15-g29: The gerund “chinando” is circumstantial and simultaneous with “rispuosi”: “while lowering/bending.” “A la” is the analytic older form of modern “alla,” and the phrase pictures Dante lowering his hand toward Brunetto’s face.
segments.inferno-15-g30: “Rispuosi” is an archaic form of “risposi,” first-person past definite, “I answered.” It is the second main verb governed by the earlier subject “io.”
segments.inferno-15-g31: “Siete voi qui” is a direct question using the polite plural “voi.” “Siete” is the present of “essere,” here meaning “are located,” since Dante is asking in astonishment whether Brunetto is in this place.
segments.inferno-15-g32: “Ser” is a respectful title, roughly “Messer” or “Sir,” used before Brunetto’s name. The vocative identifies the recognized soul as Brunetto Latini.
segments.inferno-15-g33: This segment contains only a full stop, separating the preceding exchange from the next speech; there are no lexical items to analyze.
segments.inferno-15-g34: “E quelli” literally means “and that one,” a common narrative way to resume the other speaker; the verb “said” is understood from the colon. “Quelli” refers back to Brunetto.
segments.inferno-15-g35: “O” marks direct address, and “figliuol” is an apocopated poetic form of “figliuolo,” “son.” “Mio” makes the address affectionate and paternal.
segments.inferno-15-g36: “Non ti dispiaccia” is a polite negative wish or request using the present subjunctive: “let it not displease you.” The “se” clause gives what Dante is asked to tolerate: Brunetto speaks of himself in the third person, “Brunetto Latino,” “ritorna ’n dietro” means “turns back,” and “lascia andar” uses “lasciare” plus infinitive to mean “lets go on.”
segments.inferno-15-g130: “Gente” is a collective singular noun, so the verb appears as singular “vien,” an apocopated poetic form of “viene,” “is coming.” “Con la quale” is a relative phrase referring back to “gente,” and “esser non deggio” uses the infinitive “esser” with archaic “deggio” = “I must,” with poetic word order placing “non” before the modal idea: “with whom I must not be.”
segments.inferno-15-g131: “Sieti” is an archaic fused form meaning “sia ti,” literally “may it be to you,” so the clause is optative/subjunctive: “may my Treasure be commended to you.” “Raccomandato” is a past participle agreeing with “il mio Tesoro,” which here names Brunetto’s literary work, not merely a treasure in the ordinary sense.
segments.inferno-15-g132: “Nel qual” means “in which,” with “qual” referring back to “il mio Tesoro.” “Io vivo ancora” means “I still live,” expressing that his memory or identity survives in that work; “vivo” is verbal here, though the supplied sense list gives only adjectival meanings.
segments.inferno-15-g133: “E” coordinates this final clause with the preceding request. “Più non cheggio” has poetic word order for “non chiedo più”: “I ask no more,” with archaic “cheggio” meaning “I ask.”
segments.inferno-15-g134: “Poi” marks the next action, “then.” “Si rivolse” is reflexive/intransitive, literally “he turned himself,” meaning he turned away or turned back to go.
segments.inferno-15-g135: “Parve di coloro che...” means “he seemed [to be] of those who...,” that is, “he seemed like one of those who.” The relative clause “che corrono a Verona il drappo verde per la campagna” describes runners at Verona competing for the green cloth, with “il drappo verde” functioning as the object/prize of “corrono,” and “per la campagna” meaning across the field or countryside.
segments.inferno-15-g136: “E parve di costoro” continues the comparison: “and among these people he seemed...” “Quelli che vince” is an archaic/poetic form for “the one who wins,” with “vince” in the present tense giving the image vivid immediacy.
segments.inferno-15-g137: This negative phrase completes the comparison by contrast: he seemed the winner, “not the one who loses.” “Colui che” means “he who” or “the one who,” and “perde” is present tense parallel to “vince.”
segments.inferno-15-g37: “I’” is an apocopated form of “io,” “I,” though the supplied lemma does not reflect that. “Dissi” is the first-person singular past historic, and “lui” functions here as an indirect object, “to him,” a common older/poetic usage.
segments.inferno-15-g38: “Quanto posso” means “as much as I can”; “quanto” introduces the measure or extent of Dante’s willingness. “Posso” is present indicative of “potere,” referring to his present ability.
segments.inferno-15-g39: “Ven” is contracted from “ve ne,” literally “you of it/for it,” and belongs with “preco.” “Preco” is an archaic form of “prego,” “I beg/pray,” so the phrase means “I beg you for it.”
segments.inferno-15-g40: “E se volete che…” means “and if you wish that…,” with “se” here meaning “if,” not the sense listed in the glossary. “M’asseggia” is a poetic subjunctive/archaic form meaning “that I sit myself down,” with the reflexive “m’” and the prepositional phrase “con voi,” “with you.”
segments.inferno-15-g41: “Faròl” is a contraction of “farò lo,” “I will do it.” It picks up the preceding request: Dante says he will sit down with Brunetto if permitted.
segments.inferno-15-g42: Here “se” again means “if,” though that sense is not offered in the list. “Piace a costui” means “it pleases this man,” referring to Virgil, and “che vo seco” is a relative clause: “with whom I go,” with “seco” meaning “with him.”
segments.inferno-15-g43: “O” is the vocative particle “oh,” introducing direct address. “Figliuol” is an apocopated form of “figliuolo,” “son,” used affectionately by Brunetto toward Dante.
segments.inferno-15-g44: “Disse” is the third-person singular past historic of “dire,” “he said.” It frames Brunetto’s direct speech.
segments.inferno-15-g45: “Qual di questa greggia” means “whichever one of this flock/herd,” referring collectively to the damned souls running with Brunetto. “S’arresta punto” uses the reflexive “s’” with “arresta,” “stops,” while “punto” has the force of “at all” or “even for a moment.”
segments.inferno-15-g46: “Giace poi cent’ anni” gives the punishment: “then lies for a hundred years.” “Sanz’ arrostarsi” is an archaic and context-specific phrase meaning not simply “without being roasted,” but “without warding off/fanning away the burning”; “quando ’l foco il feggia” means “when the fire strikes/does harm to him,” with “il” as an object pronoun, not the article.
segments.inferno-15-g47: “Però” here means “therefore,” drawing a conclusion from the rule that the damned must keep moving. “Va oltre” is an imperative addressed to Dante: “go on,” “go further.”
segments.inferno-15-g48: “I’” is again apocopated “io,” “I.” “Ti verrò a’ panni” literally means “I will come to your garments,” an idiom for following close beside or just behind someone, as though at the hem of his clothes.
segments.inferno-15-g49: “E poi” marks the next action: “and then.” “Rigiugnerò” is future tense, “I shall rejoin,” and “la mia masnada” means “my band/company,” with a somewhat rough or collective coloring.
segments.inferno-15-g50: “Che” introduces a relative clause referring to “masnada.” “Va piangendo” is a progressive or continuative construction, literally “goes weeping,” meaning “goes along lamenting,” and “i suoi etterni danni” refers to the company’s everlasting sufferings or losses.
segments.inferno-15-g51: “Io non osava” uses the imperfect tense: “I did not dare/was not daring.” “Scender de la strada” means “to descend from the road,” and “per andar par di lui” means “in order to go on a level with him”; here “par” is from “pari,” “equal/level,” not the verb “seems.”
segments.inferno-15-g52: “Ma ’l capo chino tenea” has poetic word order: literally “but the bowed head I held,” with “tenea” for “tenevo,” “I kept/held.” “Com’ uom che reverente vada” means “like a man who goes reverently”; “vada” is subjunctive in a comparative/generalizing clause.
segments.inferno-15-g1: “Ora” means “now,” introducing the new scene. “Cen” is the fused poetic form of “ce ne,” here meaning “us along/from there,” and “l’un de’ duri margini” is the subject: “one of the hard banks” carries the travelers.
segments.inferno-15-g2: “’l fummo” is an archaic spelling for “il fumo,” not the verb “we were”: it means the smoke or vapor. “Del ruscel” means “of/from the little stream,” while “di sopra” adverbially means “from above,” and “aduggia” has the present-tense sense “overshadows/darkens.”
segments.inferno-15-g3: “Sì che” forms a result clause, “so that.” “Dal foco” means “from the fire,” and “salva” is adjectival/predicative in force: the vapor keeps “l’acqua e li argini,” the water and banks, safe.
segments.inferno-15-g4: “Quali” begins an extended comparison and means “as,” not an ordinary question word here. The Flemings are placed geographically “tra Guizzante e Bruggia,” between Wissant and Bruges.
segments.inferno-15-g5: “Temendo” is a present participle giving the reason for their action: “fearing.” “Che ’nver’ lor s’avventa” is a relative clause modifying “fiotto”; “’nver’” means “against/toward,” and “s’avventa” is reflexive in form, “rushes/hurls itself.”
segments.inferno-15-g6: “Fanno lo schermo” means “make/build the barrier or protection.” “Perché” introduces purpose, “so that,” and “si fuggia” is a subjunctive form expressing the desired result: that the sea withdraw or flee back.
segments.inferno-15-g7: This continues the parallel comparison with “e quali,” again meaning “and as.” “Padoan” is a plural poetic form for the Paduans, and “lungo la Brenta” means “along the Brenta River.”
segments.inferno-15-g8: “Per difender” is an infinitive phrase of purpose: “in order to defend.” “Lor” is the possessive “their,” used twice before the paired nouns “ville” and “castelli.”
segments.inferno-15-g9: “Anzi che” means “before,” introducing a temporal clause. “Carentana” is Carinthia, and “senta” is present subjunctive after the temporal conjunction, meaning “feels” the heat.
segments.inferno-15-g10: “A tale imagine” means “in/to such a likeness,” tying the simile back to the embankments in Hell. “Eran fatti” is a passive construction, “were made,” and “quelli” refers back to those banks or margins.
segments.inferno-15-g11: “Tutto che” functions as a concessive conjunction, “although.” The paired negatives “né ... né ...” mean “neither ... nor,” and “sì” before each adjective means “so”: not so high and not so thick as the earthly dikes.
segments.inferno-15-g12: “Qual che si fosse” is an indefinite concessive phrase, “whatever he/it may have been.” “Fosse” is subjunctive, fitting the indefinite sense of “whatever.”
segments.inferno-15-g13: “Lo maestro” means “the master,” with “lo” as an older article form. “Félli” is not the adjective “wicked”; it is a contraction of “fé’ li,” meaning “made them,” with “li” referring to the embankments.
segments.inferno-15-g14: “Già” here means “by then,” marking the distance already traveled. “Eravam ... rimossi” is a pluperfect-like state with “were removed/gone away,” and “tanto” sets up the following result clause: “so far that.”
segments.inferno-15-g15: “Ch’i’” contracts “che io,” introducing the result of “tanto.” “Avrei visto” is conditional perfect, “I would have seen,” and “dov’ era” means “where it was,” referring to the forest.
segments.inferno-15-g16: “Perch’” has a concessive force here, roughly “even if,” rather than “because.” “In dietro rivolto mi fossi” is a pluperfect subjunctive reflexive/passive-like phrase: “I had turned myself backward”; the listed “fossi” is really from “essere,” not the noun “ditches.”
segments.inferno-15-g17: “Quando” introduces the main event after the preceding distance description. “Incontrammo d’anime una schiera” uses poetic word order for “we met a troop of souls,” and “che venian lungo l’argine” is a relative clause: “who were coming along the bank.”
segments.inferno-15-g18: “Ciascuna” refers to each soul in the troop. “Ci riguardava” means “looked at us,” and the comparison “come suol ... guardare uno altro” means “as one person is accustomed to look at another,” especially in the dim light “under a new moon.”
segments.inferno-15-g19: “Sì ... come” forms another comparison: “so ... as.” “Ver’ noi” is poetic for “verso noi,” “toward us,” and “aguzzavan le ciglia” literally means “sharpened their eyelashes/eyes,” idiomatically “strained their gaze,” like an old tailor peering at a needle’s eye.
segments.inferno-15-g20: “Così” means “thus,” referring back to the intense scrutiny just described. “Adocchiato” is a past participle, “eyed/observed,” and “da cotal famiglia” is an agent phrase: “by such a company.”
segments.inferno-15-g21: “Fui conosciuto” is a passive perfect: “I was recognized.” “Da un” means “by one,” with “un” standing substantively for one member of the group.
segments.inferno-15-g22: “Che” is a relative pronoun referring to the one who recognized him. “Mi prese per lo lembo” means “seized me by the hem,” where “per” expresses the part grasped, and “gridò” adds the second action, “cried out.”
segments.inferno-15-g23: “Qual” is exclamatory here: “What!” rather than a genuine question. “Maraviglia” is the older spelling of “meraviglia,” meaning “marvel” or “wonder.”
segments.inferno-15-g84: The subject is “La tua fortuna,” meaning Dante’s destined fortune or fate, not merely good luck. “Ti serba” places the indirect object before the verb: it “reserves for you” such great honor; “onor” is a poetic shortened form of “onore.”
segments.inferno-15-g85: “Che” introduces the result of “tanto”: so much honor that both opposing “parts” or political sides will desire Dante. “Avranno fame di te” literally means “will have hunger for you,” an idiom here used metaphorically for craving or wanting him.
segments.inferno-15-g86: “Ma” contrasts their desire with their inability to obtain him. “Fia” is an archaic future form of “essere,” meaning “will be,” and the proverb-like image “the grass will be far from the beak” means the desired food will remain out of reach.
segments.inferno-15-g87: “Faccian” is an archaic third-person plural present subjunctive used jussively: “let them make.” The phrase “strame di lor medesme” means “litter/refuse of their very selves,” with “lor medesme” functioning as an emphatic reflexive.
segments.inferno-15-g88: “Tocchin” is another archaic plural subjunctive continuing the command: “and let them not touch.” “La pianta” is the metaphorical plant that must be spared, linked to the noble Roman seed mentioned next.
segments.inferno-15-g89: Here “s’” is the contraction of “se,” meaning “if,” not a reflexive pronoun. “Alcuna” is elliptical, referring back to “pianta”: “if any plant still springs up”; “lor” means “their,” referring to the Fiesolan beasts.
segments.inferno-15-g90: “In cui” refers back to the plant: in that plant the “sementa santa,” the sacred lineage, may live again. “Riviva” is subjunctive, dependent on the wish-like construction, and “fu fatto” is a passive perfect: “was made,” describing Florence as the “nest” of great wickedness.
segments.inferno-15-g91: “Se fosse” introduces a contrary-to-fact conditional clause with the imperfect subjunctive: “if it were/had been.” “Tutto pieno” means fully satisfied or fulfilled, and “il mio dimando” is Dante’s wish or request.
segments.inferno-15-g92: “Rispuos’” is an apocopated poetic form of “rispuosi,” “I replied.” The word order “rispuos’ io lui” is literary, with “lui” as the object: “I replied to him.”
segments.inferno-15-g93: This is the main clause of the unreal condition: “you would not yet be...” “Sareste” is conditional, and “posto in bando” is an idiom meaning “banished”; “de l’umana natura” means “from human nature,” that is, from mortal human life.
segments.inferno-15-g94: “Ché” means “because/for,” giving the reason for Dante’s wish. “’n la” is a poetic contraction for “in la,” and “m’è fitta” literally means “is fixed to me,” with “m’” as a dative of personal involvement.
segments.inferno-15-g95: “Or” means “now,” contrasting present grief with remembered affection. “M’accora” means “it distresses me” or “grieves my heart,” with “m’” as the direct object.
segments.inferno-15-g96: This noun phrase is delayed until after the preceding predicates: the thing fixed in Dante’s mind and grieving him is “la cara e buona imagine paterna di voi.” “M’insegnavate” is imperfect, describing Brunetto’s repeated teaching in life, and “come l’uom s’etterna” means “how man makes himself eternal,” through fame or enduring works.
segments.inferno-15-g97: “Quant’” means “how much,” introducing an indirect exclamatory clause. “L’abbia in grado” is an idiom meaning “I hold it dear,” “I value it,” or “I am grateful for it”; “abbia” is subjunctive because the clause depends on “quant’.”
segments.inferno-15-g98: “Mentr’ io vivo” means “while I live,” with poetic elision in “mentr’.” “Convien che” means “it is necessary that,” followed by the subjunctive “si scerna”; “ne la mia lingua” means “in my speech,” so Dante says his gratitude must be evident in his words.
segments.inferno-15-g99: “Ciò che” means “that which” or “what,” serving as the object of “scrivo.” “Narrate” is second-person plural, addressed respectfully to Brunetto, and “di mio corso” means concerning the course of Dante’s life or destiny.
segments.inferno-15-g100: “Serbolo” combines “serbo” and “lo”: “I keep/reserve it.” “A chiosar” means “for glossing/commenting on,” and “a donna che saprà” means the matter is reserved for a lady who will know how to explain it, namely Beatrice.
segments.inferno-15-g101: Here “s’” is the contraction of “se,” meaning “if.” “A lei arrivo” uses “arrivare a” in the sense “to reach her,” with “arrivo” functioning as the first-person present verb, not as a noun.
segments.inferno-15-g102: “Tanto” is placed first for emphasis and anticipates the following “ch’…” clause: “this much, namely that….” “Vogl’ io” is an elided form of “voglio io,” with the subject pronoun expressed for emphasis, and “che vi sia manifesto” uses the present subjunctive “sia” after a verb of wishing: “that it be clear to you.”
segments.inferno-15-g103: “Pur che” forms a conditional proviso, meaning “provided that” or “so long as.” “Mi garra” has “mi” as the object, and “garra” is a subjunctive form of an archaic verb meaning “rebuke” or “scold,” governed by the proviso.
segments.inferno-15-g104: “Ch’” introduces the content of what Dante wants to make clear: “that….” “A la Fortuna” means “for/to Fortune,” with Fortune personified as a power to which he is prepared to submit or respond.
segments.inferno-15-g105: “Come” means “as,” introducing the manner in which Fortune may act. “Vuol” is the shortened present form of “vuole,” here with the sense “wills.”
segments.inferno-15-g106: “Son” is the first-person singular present form of “essere,” shortened from “sono.” “Presto” here is adjectival, meaning “ready,” not the adverb “soon.”
segments.inferno-15-g107: The subject is “tal arra,” placed after the predicate adjective “nuova”; the phrase means that such a token or pledge is not new to Dante. “A li orecchi miei” is an indirect construction, literally “to my ears,” where modern Italian would use “agli orecchi miei.”
segments.inferno-15-g108: “Però” means “therefore,” drawing a conclusion from the preceding statement. “Giri” is present subjunctive used jussively: “let Fortune turn”; “come le piace” uses “piacere” impersonally with “le” as a dative pronoun, “as it pleases her.”
segments.inferno-15-g109: This segment is elliptical: the verb “giri” is understood again, so the sense is “and let the peasant turn his hoe.” “’l” is the contracted article “il,” and “villan” is an apocopated form of “villano,” meaning a peasant or rustic.
segments.inferno-15-g110: “Lo mio maestro” is an archaic form of “il mio maestro.” “Si volse in dietro” is reflexive in form and means “turned himself backward,” while “in su la gota destra” describes the physical direction of the turn, over or toward the right cheek; “riguardommi” is “riguardò mi,” with the object pronoun enclitic, “looked back at me.”
segments.inferno-15-g111: “Poi” marks the next action in the sequence after Virgil turns and looks at Dante. “Disse” is the simple past, third-person singular of “dire.”
segments.inferno-15-g112: The saying is gnomic: “chi” means “he who” or “whoever.” “La nota” uses “nota” as a verb from “notare,” “takes note of it,” with “la” as a feminine object pronoun referring to the lesson or remark just made.
segments.inferno-15-g113: “Né per tanto di men” means “nor any less for that,” that is, Dante is not deterred by Virgil’s warning glance. “Vommi” is a contracted form of “vo mi,” from “andare,” meaning “I go on” or “I continue on my way,” and “parlando con ser Brunetto” is a gerundial phrase describing what he is doing as he goes.
segments.inferno-15-g114: “Dimando” is an old form of “domando,” “I ask,” though the supplied lemma is misleading. “Chi sono” introduces an indirect question, and “li suoi compagni più noti e più sommi” is the subject of “sono,” with “più” forming superlatives: “the most known and most eminent.”
segments.inferno-15-g62: “Pur” here has the limiting sense “only/just,” and “ier” is the poetic shortened form of “ieri,” “yesterday.” In “le volsi le spalle,” the first “le” is a dative pronoun referring back to the valley, while the second “le” is the article in the idiom “volgere le spalle a,” to turn one’s back on.
segments.inferno-15-g63: “Questi” is a demonstrative pronoun meaning “this man,” referring to the guide present with Dante. “M’apparve” is “mi apparve,” with elision, and the passato remoto verb means “appeared to me.”
segments.inferno-15-g64: “Tornand’” is the gerund “tornando,” with its final vowel elided before “ïo”; the phrase means “while/as I was returning.” “In quella” means “into that one,” referring again to the valley, and the explicit “ïo” clarifies the subject of the gerund.
segments.inferno-15-g65: “Reducemi” is a poetic form of “reduce mi,” with the object pronoun attached after the verb, meaning “leads me back.” “A ca” is a shortened poetic form for “a casa,” “home,” and “per questo calle” means “by/through this path.”
segments.inferno-15-g66: This is an elliptical speech formula: “And he to me” means “And he said to me,” with the verb of speaking omitted. “Elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” “he,” though the supplied senses do not list that pronoun value.
segments.inferno-15-g67: “Se” means “if” here, introducing a real or general condition, though that sense is not present in the provided list. “Segui tua stella” uses the present indicative and the metaphor of following one’s guiding star, that is, one’s destined course.
segments.inferno-15-g68: “Non puoi” governs the infinitive “fallire”: literally “you cannot fail/miss.” The phrase “fallire a glorïoso porto” means “fail to reach a glorious harbor,” where “porto” is a metaphor for a successful end or destination.
segments.inferno-15-g69: “Se ben” means “if rightly/well,” qualifying Brunetto’s judgment. “M’accorsi” is “mi accorsi,” passato remoto of “accorgersi,” “I perceived/realized,” and “ne la vita bella” means “in the beautiful life,” that is, earthly life.
segments.inferno-15-g70: “S’io” is elision of “se io,” “if I.” “Fossi” is the imperfect subjunctive of “essere” in a contrary-to-fact condition, and “sì per tempo morto” means “dead so early / before my time.”
segments.inferno-15-g71: “Veggendo” is a gerund, “seeing,” giving the circumstance or reason for what Brunetto would have done. “Il cielo a te così benigno” means “heaven so favorable toward you,” with “cielo” carrying an astrological or providential sense.
segments.inferno-15-g72: The normal order would be “t’avrei dato conforto”; Dante places “dato” first for poetic emphasis and meter. “T’” is the indirect object “to you,” and “a l’opera” means “for the work/undertaking.”
segments.inferno-15-g73: “Ma quello ingrato popolo maligno” is the subject of the later verb “ti si farà.” The relative clause “che discese di Fiesole ab antico” says that this people descended from Fiesole in ancient times; “ab antico” is a Latinizing expression meaning “from of old.”
segments.inferno-15-g74: This clause continues the description of the same people. “Tiene ancor del monte e del macigno” uses “del” partitively: they still retain something of the mountain and the hard rock, metaphorically their rough, stony nature.
segments.inferno-15-g75: “Ti” is a dative pronoun, “to/against you.” “Si farà” is a pronominal future form meaning “will make itself / will become,” with the subject being the “popolo” mentioned earlier.
segments.inferno-15-g76: “Per” gives the cause: “because of.” “Tuo ben far” is a substantivized infinitive phrase, literally “your well-doing,” meaning “your doing good.”
segments.inferno-15-g77: “Nimico” is an archaic form of “nemico.” It functions as a predicate complement after “si farà”: the people will become an enemy or hostile to Dante.
segments.inferno-15-g78: “Ed è ragion” means “and it is right/reasonable,” with “ragion” shortened from “ragione.” The clause judges the hostility as fitting within the metaphor that follows.
segments.inferno-15-g79: “Ché” introduces the explanation, “for/because.” “Si disconvien” is impersonal, “it is unfitting,” and “al dolce fico” is a dative construction: it is unsuitable for the sweet fig tree to bear fruit among sour sorb-trees.
segments.inferno-15-g80: “Vecchia fama” is the subject: an old report or long-standing reputation. “Li chiama orbi” has “li” as the direct object pronoun “them” and “orbi” as the object complement, “calls them blind.”
segments.inferno-15-g81: “Gent’” is an elided form of “gente” before “è.” Although it refers to a people collectively, it takes a singular verb and singular feminine adjective: “is greedy.”
segments.inferno-15-g82: The adjectives continue the predicate describing “gent’.” “Invidiosa” and “superba” are feminine singular to agree with “gente,” understood from the previous segment.
segments.inferno-15-g83: “Dai lor costumi” means “from their customs/ways,” with “lor” as the possessive “their.” “Fa che tu ti forbi” is an imperative idiom, “see to it that you cleanse yourself,” with “ti” reflexive and “forbi” used morally: keep yourself clean of their conduct.
segments.inferno-15-g115: This is an elliptical speech tag: the verb “said” is understood from context. “elli” is an archaic form of “egli,” meaning “he,” and “a me” marks the indirect object, “to me.”
segments.inferno-15-g116: “Saper” is an infinitive used as a noun, “knowing,” and it is the subject of “è buono.” “d’alcuno” literally means “of some,” here “about some of them”; “buono” is the adjective “good,” though the supplied lemma/senses do not match that use.
segments.inferno-15-g117: “de li altri” means “about the others,” with the separated old form of the article “li.” “fia” is an archaic future form, “will be,” and “tacerci” is an infinitive with the particle “ci,” meaning roughly “to keep silent here/about them.”
segments.inferno-15-g118: “ché” introduces the reason: “for/because.” “saria” is an archaic conditional, modern Italian “sarebbe,” and “a tanto suono” means “for so much sound,” that is, for so much talking or naming.
segments.inferno-15-g119: “In somma” is an idiom meaning “in sum” or “in short,” not literally “in the highest.” “sappi” is the imperative of “sapere,” “know,” followed by a “che”-clause; “fur” is the shortened poetic form of “furono,” “were.”
segments.inferno-15-g120: This segment completes the description of “tutti”: they were “lerci,” filthy or stained, “d’un peccato medesmo,” by one same sin. “al mondo” means “in the world,” that is, during their earthly lives.
segments.inferno-15-g121: “Priscian” is the subject, and “sen va” is “se ne va,” a pronominal form meaning “goes off/goes along.” “quella turba grama” means “that wretched crowd”; “turba” here is a crowd, though the supplied senses do not include that meaning.
segments.inferno-15-g122: This adds a second named figure to the same group. “d’Accorso” is a patronymic or family designation, “of Accorso,” and “anche” means “also/too.”
segments.inferno-15-g123: “vedervi” is “vedere” plus “vi,” meaning “to see there.” The infinitive depends on the later “potei,” so the construction is interrupted by the conditional clause that follows.
segments.inferno-15-g124: Here “s’” is not reflexive but the elided conjunction “se,” “if.” “avessi avuto” is the pluperfect subjunctive, “if I had had,” and “di tal tigna brama” means “a desire for such scab/scurf,” a contemptuous metaphor for such vile company.
segments.inferno-15-g125: “colui” is the object of the earlier “vedervi”: “I could have seen that one.” “che” begins a relative clause; “fu trasmutato” is a passive perfect, with “dal servo de’ servi” as agent, the papal title “Servant of the servants,” and “d’Arno in Bacchiglione” means transferred from the region of the Arno to that of the Bacchiglione.
segments.inferno-15-g126: “dove” refers back to “Bacchiglione,” the place to which he was transferred. “lasciò” is a simple past, and “li mal protesi nervi” literally means “the badly stretched sinews,” a harsh physical image for his death and corrupted body.
segments.inferno-15-g127: “Di più” means “more of it” or simply “more.” “direi” is the conditional of “dire,” “I would say,” suggesting willingness checked by the limitation stated next.
segments.inferno-15-g128: “’l venire e ’l sermone” uses substantivized infinitive/noun phrases: “the going/walking and the discourse.” The word order is poetic: “più lungo esser non può” corresponds to normal prose “non può esser più lungo,” “cannot be longer.”
segments.inferno-15-g129: “però ch’” functions together as a causal conjunction, “because.” “i’” is an elided form of “io,” “I,” “veggio” is an archaic form of “vedo,” and “surger nuovo fummo del sabbione” means “new smoke rising from the sandy expanse.”

sentences.inferno-15-s13: He began: “What fortune or destiny leads you down here before your final day?
sentences.inferno-15-s14: and who is this one who shows the way?”
sentences.inferno-15-s16: “Up there above, in the serene life,” I replied to him, “I lost my way in a valley, before my age was full.
sentences.inferno-15-s6: And I, when he stretched his arm toward me, fixed my eyes through his scorched appearance, so that his burned face did not prevent my understanding from recognizing him; and lowering my hand toward his face, I answered: “Are you here, ser Brunetto?”
sentences.inferno-15-s8: And he said: “O my son, do not be displeased if Brunetto Latini turns back a little with you and lets the trail go on.”
sentences.inferno-15-s33: People are coming with whom I must not be.
sentences.inferno-15-s34: Let my Treasure be commended to you, in which I still live; and I ask no more.
sentences.inferno-15-s35: Then he turned back, and seemed like one of those who run for the green cloth at Verona across the field; and among these he seemed the one who wins, not the one who loses.
sentences.inferno-15-s9: I said to him: “As much as I can, I beg you for it; and if you wish me to sit down with you, I will do it, if it pleases this man with whom I go.”
sentences.inferno-15-s10: “O my son,” he said, “whoever of this herd stops even for a moment must then lie for a hundred years, without warding off the burning when the fire strikes him.”
sentences.inferno-15-s11: Therefore go on: I will come along at your skirts; and then I shall rejoin my band, which goes lamenting its eternal losses.”
sentences.inferno-15-s12: I did not dare descend from the road in order to go level with him; but I kept my head bowed, like a man who walks reverently.
sentences.inferno-15-s1: Now one of the hard banks carries us along, and the vapor from the stream above overshadows it, so that it keeps the water and the banks safe from the fire.
sentences.inferno-15-s2: As the Flemings between Wissant and Bruges, fearing the surge that rushes against them, build the barrier so that the sea may withdraw; and as the Paduans along the Brenta do, to defend their villages and their castles before Carinthia feels the heat: in such a likeness were those banks made, though they were neither so high nor so thick, whatever master made them.
sentences.inferno-15-s3: By then we had gone so far away from the wood that I would not have seen where it was, even if I had turned back, when we came upon a troop of souls who were coming along the bank; and each one gazed at us as people at evening under a new moon are wont to look at one another; and they strained their eyes toward us as an old tailor does at the eye of a needle.
sentences.inferno-15-s4: Thus eyed by such a company, I was recognized by one of them, who seized me by the hem and cried out, “What a marvel!”
sentences.inferno-15-s21: Your fortune reserves so much honor for you that both one side and the other will hunger for you; but the grass will be far from the beak.
sentences.inferno-15-s22: Let the Fiesolan beasts make litter of themselves, and let them not touch the plant, if any still springs up in their dung, in which the holy seed of those Romans may live again who remained there when the nest of such great wickedness was made.
sentences.inferno-15-s23: “If my request had been wholly fulfilled,” I replied to him, “you would not yet have been placed in banishment from human nature; for fixed in my mind—and now it grieves my heart—is the dear and good fatherly image of you, when in the world, from time to time, you taught me how man makes himself eternal; and how much I hold it in gratitude, while I live, must be discerned in my speech.
sentences.inferno-15-s24: What you tell me about my course I write down, and I reserve it to be glossed with another text by a lady who will know, if I reach her.
sentences.inferno-15-s25: This much I want to be clear to you: provided my conscience does not reproach me, I am ready for Fortune, as she wills.
sentences.inferno-15-s26: No such pledge is unfamiliar to my ears; therefore let Fortune turn her wheel as she pleases, and let the peasant turn his hoe.
sentences.inferno-15-s27: Then my master turned backward over his right cheek and looked back at me; then he said: “He listens well who takes note of it.”
sentences.inferno-15-s28: Nor, for all that, do I go on any the less talking with Sir Brunetto, and I ask who his best-known and most eminent companions are.
sentences.inferno-15-s17: Only yesterday morning I turned my back on it; this one appeared to me as I was returning into it, and he is leading me back home by this path.
sentences.inferno-15-s18: And he said to me: If you follow your star, you cannot fail to reach a glorious harbor, if I discerned rightly in the fair life; and if I had not died so before my time, seeing heaven so favorable to you, I would have given you encouragement in the work.
sentences.inferno-15-s19: But that ungrateful, malicious people, who came down from Fiesole of old and still retain something of the mountain and of the hard stone, will become your enemy because of your doing good; and it is right, for among the sour sorb-trees it is unfitting for the sweet fig tree to bear fruit.
sentences.inferno-15-s20: Old report in the world calls them blind; they are a greedy, envious, and proud people: see that you cleanse yourself of their ways.
sentences.inferno-15-s29: And he said to me: “It is good to know about some; as for the others, it will be praiseworthy for us to keep silent, for the time would be too short for so much speech.”
sentences.inferno-15-s30: In short, know that all of them were clerics and great men of letters, and of great fame, filthy in the world with one and the same sin.
sentences.inferno-15-s31: Priscian goes along with that wretched crowd, and Francesco d’Accorso too; and I could have seen there, if I had had any desire for such scurf, the one who was transferred by the Servant of the servants from the Arno to the Bacchiglione, where he left his badly strained sinews.
sentences.inferno-15-s32: I would say more; but the walking and the discourse cannot be any longer, because I see new smoke rising there from the sandy plain.
